title: ‘The photograph is handsome, as is the boy’: the ‘hot felony’ of Luigi Mangione
abstract: This essay explores the transformation of ‘CEO killer’ Luigi Mangione into a ‘hot felon’. It argues that the public desire for Mangione derives from the online currency of his mugshot and the ways in which it lends itself to a digital ‘face culture’ of celebrity, sexual appraisal and public judgement. Exploring the explosion of memes that position Mangione as a sex object, the essay examines what is at stake in the hyper-sexualised focus on him and how that relates to his wider valorisation as a white folk hero and patron saint of healthcare justice in the United States. Through an analysis of the vibes and affects generated by Mangione memes and photographs, I explore the ways in which his online recognisability, and the attendant sympathy produced for him, is inextricably tied to his idealised whiteness and masculinity.
text:
Celebrity Studies
ISSN: 1939-2397 (Print) 1939-2400 (Online) Journal homepage: www.tandfonline.com/journals/rcel20
‘The photograph is handsome, as is the boy’: the
‘hot felony’ of Luigi Mangione
Tanya Horeck
To cite this article: Tanya Horeck (17 Nov 2025): ‘The photograph is handsome, as is the boy’:
the ‘hot felony’ of Luigi Mangione, Celebrity Studies, DOI: 10.1080/19392397.2025.2578493

‘The photograph is handsome, as is the boy’: the ‘hot felony’ 
of Luigi Mangione
Tanya Horeck
Cambridge School of Creative Industries, Anglia Ruskin University, Cambridge, UK
ABSTRACT
This essay explores the transformation of ‘CEO killer’ Luigi 
Mangione into a ‘hot felon’. It argues that the public desire for 
Mangione derives from the online currency of his mugshot and 
the ways in which it lends itself to a digital ‘face culture’ of celebrity, 
sexual appraisal and public judgement. Exploring the explosion of 
memes that position Mangione as a sex object, the essay examines 
what is at stake in the hyper-sexualised focus on him and how that 
relates to his wider valorisation as a white folk hero and patron saint 
of healthcare justice in the United States. Through an analysis of the 
vibes and affects generated by Mangione memes and photographs, 
I explore the ways in which his online recognisability, and the 
attendant sympathy produced for him, is inextricably tied to his 
idealised whiteness and masculinity.
ARTICLE HISTORY 
Received 30 May 2025  
Accepted 17 October 2025 
KEYWORDS 
Hot felons; mugshots; 
criminality; masculinity; 
whiteness
What is peculiar to modern societies, in fact, is not that they consigned sex to a shadow 
existence, but that they dedicated themselves to speaking of it ad infinitum, while exploiting 
it as the secret. 
– Michel Foucault, The History of Sexuality: Volume 1
‘If the guy is fit, you must acquit.’ X user
On 4 December 2024, an unidentified shooter killed 50-year-old Brian Thompson, the white 
male CEO of UnitedHealthcare, on a sidewalk in Manhattan. Before anyone even knew who 
the at-large suspect was, or what he looked like, a dominant online response to the murder 
was one of palpable elation. In a New Yorker report, published on 7 December 2024, titled ‘A 
Man Was Murdered in Cold Blood and You Are Laughing? What the Death of a Health 
Insurance CEO Means to America’, Jia Tolentino describes the public response to the murder 
as swift, wild and ‘lawless’ (2024). She discusses how internet jokes were immediate, came 
from various sides of the political fence and were shot through with fury over the ‘structural 
violence’ of the capitalist healthcare industry in the United States. As Tolentino reported: ‘On 
LinkedIn, where users post with their real names and employment histories, UnitedHealth 
Group had to turn off comments on its post about Thompson’s death – thousands of people 
were liking and hearting it, with a few even giving it the “clapping” reaction’ (2024).
CONTACT Tanya Horeck 
tanya.horeck@aru.ac.uk
CELEBRITY STUDIES                                         
https://doi.org/10.1080/19392397.2025.2578493
© 2025 The Author(s). Published by Informa UK Limited, trading as Taylor & Francis Group.  
This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License (http://creativecommons.org/

cited. The terms on which this article has been published allow the posting of the Accepted Manuscript in a repository by the author(s) or 
with their consent.
A nationwide manhunt ended five days later when local police in Altoona, 
Pennsylvania, arrested the suspected gunman, 26 year-old Luigi Mangione, at a 
McDonald’s after receiving a tip from an employee who recognised Mangione from 
media coverage of the shooting (Rubin 2024). Young, white, and handsome, 
Mangione’s face was the perfect, idealised interface-object for many on the internet to 
attach their feelings to, and a folk hero was born. Mangione, an Ivy League graduate from 
a wealthy Italian American family in Baltimore, was reported to be carrying a handwritten 
manifesto decrying healthcare executives as ‘parasites’ who ‘had it coming’ (Mangan  
2024). ‘Deny’, ‘defend’, ‘depose’ – words apparently used by the healthcare insurance 
industry to deny coverage to sick people in the United States, were scrawled on the shell 
casings of the bullets used (Halpert and Wendling 2024). This information fuelled and 
inspired Gen Z TikTokers who led the charge in heralding Mangione as a ‘Real Life Robin 
Hood’, driven to challenge the corporate greed of the profit-driven healthcare insurance 
industry in America (Schmunk 2024).
Reflecting a deep-seated social anger against American healthcare companies and 
capitalist greed, much of the social media discourse hailed Mangione as a hero while users 
combed through his digital footprint to uncover his personal history and potential 
motives.
At the same time, something else was happening: people were responding to 
his ‘Italian good looks’ and transforming him into a sex symbol for our times. 
Mangione’s mugshot, along with photos from his social media profiles – including 
one of him topless while hiking – were shared and viewed by millions (Figures 1). 
Generative AI was used to make Mangione even more handsome in an enhanced 
Figure 1. Luigi Mangione Mugshot.
Figure 2. AI-Generated Mugshot of Mangione.
2
T. HORECK
mugshot that circulated widely and was seemingly thought to be genuine before 
Reuters flagged it as fake (Reuters Fact Check 2024) [Figure 2]. Mainstream media 
reports puzzled over the ‘thirst’ for Mangione seen across the internet, all the while 
reinforcing it through references to his ‘toothpaste ad-worthy smile’ (Savin 2024), 
his impressive ‘six-pack’ (Friedman 2024) and the stylishness of his Sherpa-lined 
Levi’s hooded jacket (which apparently became a ‘fashion phenomenon’ following 
Reddit discussion) (Kato 2024). Later, when Mangione appeared for his arraignment 
on murder charges at a courthouse in New York City, tabloids such as Britain’s The 
Daily Mail reported gushing interest in his new haircut, ‘freshly groomed eyebrows’ 
and post prison stay ‘glow-up’ across the internet (Abrams 2024).
What is at stake in the rapid online commodification of Mangione as a ‘hot felon’ or, to 
borrow from internet parlance, a ‘mugshot hottie/mugshawty’? This essay argues that the 
public desire for Mangione as an idealised white male figure derives in large part from the 
online currency of his mugshot and the ways in which it lends itself to a digital ‘face 
culture’ of celebrity, sexual appraisal and public judgement (Dinnen and McBean 2022, p. 
273). Originating as a tool for police identification and classification purposes in the 19th 
century, the mugshot was standardised by French police officer Alphonse Bertillon as an 
image of the head and shoulders of the subject, one consisting of a frontal shot of the face 
and the other a side profile shot (Clark 2015). Although they were designed to aid in police 
work, mugshots, through their close links to visibility, exposure and notoriety, have also 
always been attached to the concept of celebrity. As Lourdes Delgado has illustrated, so- 
called ‘rogues’ galleries’ of criminal portraits open to the public for viewing from the 
1850s onwards had much in common with photography galleries that displayed the 
portraits of ‘celebrated men and women’ (2017, p. 221).
The internet, and particularly the rise of social media networks post-2010, has intensi­
fied the inherent relationship between criminality and celebrity with the emergence of a 
mugshot economy. For a time, in the early 2000s, extortionate mugshot practices flour­
ished in which unscrupulous websites published mugshots without consent only to 
charge people for taking them down, before becoming banned (Mahdawi 2018). 
Following the racist police murder of George Floyd on 25 May 2020, and the rise of the 
Black Lives Matter movement, some news agencies took the decision not to publish 
mugshots any longer on the grounds of their racist history in criminalising Black people 
(Cramer 2020).
However, despite increasing conversations about the ethics of publishing and 
circulating mugshots online, they continue to hold strong appeal for a digital attention 
economy, circulating on social media platforms such as Instagram, X, and TikTok. In 
the last two decades there has been a slew of media stories that indicate how 
mugshots have become strangely absorbed into the wider online culture of self- 
branding and commodification. For example, in one widely reported case from 
Australia in 2016, an 18-year-old woman asked for a ‘more flattering mugshot’ to be 
used and sent along a preferred photo of herself to police (Furedi 2016). In another 
example from July 2019, Newsweek ran a story with the following headline: ‘Ex-Model 
Fugitive Sends a Picture to Replace the Unflattering Police Mugshot’ (Keeley 2019). In 
other words, digital-era mug shots operate according to the aspirational thrust of 
social media platforms, which train users to exploit ‘marketing techniques to boost 
attention and visibility’ (Marwick 2013, p. 12). In a neoliberal culture of constant self- 
CELEBRITY STUDIES
3
branding and consumption, mugshots circulate as commodified objects with a pecu­
liar kind of celebrity currency.
This is the context into which Luigi Mangione’s mugshot was released, with many on 
the internet ready to receive it and to elevate it in relation to an already celebrified and 
libidinized digital mugshot economy.
In the essay that follows, I analyse Luigi Mangione’s mugshot as a key vector of his 
internet celebrity, suggesting that its online circulation positions him in the manner of a 
Hollywood star – as a site of ‘meaning, desire and attraction’ (Redmond 2021, p. 5). 
Drawing from digital humanities scholarship that examines how memes serve as ‘conduits 
of affect on the internet’ (Griffin 2021, p. 4), spreading as humorous yet ideological forms 
of collective sentiment (Milner 2016, Horeck 2022, Nissenbaum and Shifman 2017, 
Shifman 2013), I unpack the cultural logics of Mangione ‘hot felon’ memes. My aim is to 
explore the role these Mangione heartthrob memes play in justifying and minimising his 
violence and establishing him as the patron saint of healthcare justice. I suggest that 
Mangione’s sexual memefication is borne out of a celebrity culture which is embedded in 
a broader ‘social media logics’ (Van Dijck and Poell 2013) that has significantly shifted the 
ways in which criminal cases are now responded to and interpreted. These logics borrow 
from a culture of surveillance and detection, as well as from a dating app/hook up swipe 
culture which is rooted in, and reproduces, gendered, raced, and sexualised norms and 
ideals about the face as a commodified digital object.
There is a political urgency to this task of exploring the ‘frames of recognition’ (Butler  
2009, p. 5) through which Mangione is understood and responded to, because, as Judith 
Butler has argued, such frames determine the norms that ‘operate to produce certain 
subjects as “recognizable” persons and to make others decidedly more difficult to recog­
nize’ (2009, p. 6). The question of ‘recognizability’ is important for thinking about which 
crimes attain public attention, and which do not, in an internet attention economy 
increasingly characterised by ‘moods and vibes’, and the sharing of sensations and 
feelings rather than critical thoughts (Lupinacci 2025). Through an analysis of the vibes 
and affects generated by Mangione memes, I explore the ways in which his online 
recognisability, and the attendant sympathy produced for him, is inextricably tied to his 
idealised whiteness and masculinity.
A white moment
In a YouTube comedy segment, ‘The Failure, Fear and Frenzy Around Luigi Mangione’,1 
which has been viewed 4.2 million times as of April 2025, Black American comedian Josh 
Johnson reflects on the justified levels of anger over the state of healthcare in America. 
Describing how he, like the rest of the world, came to learn of the murder of Thompson 
via TV news and the internet, Johnson notes that his first thought upon seeing the 
surveillance footage of a masked man cocking and pointing a gun at CEO Thompson, 
was: ‘Thank god, those are white hands’. Amidst much laughter from the audience, 
Johnson continues to riff: ‘When I saw those lily-white hands, I thought to myself . . . 
what a beautiful white thing to happen that does not have anything to do with me, that 
should not blow back on me at all . . . what a White moment in America’. The joke lands 
because the audience knows what Johnson is saying to be true: if the hands that cocked 
4
T. HORECK
that gun and fired it, murdering a white male CEO, were black or brown hands, the public 
response would be very different.
Under the terms of a racist US imaginary, Black citizens are unduly and unjustly 
criminalised and ‘incarcerated at more than 5 times the rate of whites’ (Criminal Justice 
Fact Sheet). As Simone Browne has discussed in her pathfinding book, Dark Matters: On 
the Surveillance of Blackness, ‘even the mere presence of blackness gets coded as criminal’ 
(Browne 2015, p. 20). Mugshots are a central part of the ‘racialized surveillance’ analysed 
by Browne. From the start, mugshots were racially inflected and tied to a dubious ‘science’ 
of phrenology and physiognomy that claimed to evince criminality in bodily and racial 
characteristics which produced ‘criminal types’ (Beckman 2004, p. 11). Their production 
and circulation continue to promote racist ideas, and research has shown that mugshots 
of white men and Black men are often treated differently in media stories (Noble 2018). 
The mugshots of white men tend not to be shown as frequently in media accounts of 
crimes and when they are presented, it is often in relation to other photos from their lives 
in an attempt to humanise them, whereas mugshots of Black men are shown more 
routinely and reduce Black men to criminal and murderous ‘types’ (see Marriott 2000, p. 
viii).
While blackness is hypervisible and over-surveilled,2 the power of whiteness, as Richard 
Dyer has taught us, derives from its unremarkability, the way in which it is both ‘every­
thing and nothing’ (1988, p. 49). Mangione’s whiteness is not a focal point for how he is 
generally discussed in the media, but it is fundamental to his sexualisation as a ‘hot 
assassin’, as well as to the public sympathy that has been generated for him. Though 
attempts were made on news sites such as The Guardian (Oladipo 2024), and social media 
sites such as Reddit, to unpack the racialised dimensions of the Mangione case, his 
perceived good looks have by and large displaced discussions of race. The tendency is 
for his whiteness to be implicitly assumed in discussions of his wealth, privilege and 
‘powerful family roots’ in the words of one BBC report (Parker and Pigliucci 2024).
In an important 2004 essay in Camera Obscura on femininity, whiteness and death row 
photography, Karen Beckman (now Redrobe) explores how the media represented the 
death row case of 38-year-old white woman Karla Faye Tucker, who was executed in 1998 
and became ‘a poster girl of the anti-capital-punishment groups’. Beckman notes that, 
even in those instances where attempts were made by commentators ‘to stress the 
implicit racism underlying the media’s exceptional support of Tucker, ‘race itself drops 
out of the picture’ in the focus on her ‘“sex” and “prettiness”’ (2004, p. 3). Beckman 
examines the ways in which Tucker’s prettiness is ‘racially marked’, arguing that the levels 
of ‘attention and care’ directed towards her from the US public ‘reveals how profoundly 
visual norms of acceptable femininity still shape our responses to women on death row’ 
(2004, p. 11).
While Mangione’s whiteness has been used to humanise him and informs the visual 
norms of his ‘acceptable’ masculinity, it is important to note that internet memes have 
also drawn from ethnic stereotypes to generate humour around his Italian-ness (Figure 3). 
These memes poke fun at his name – ‘Luigi Mangione sounds like what J. K. Rowling 
would name an Italian character’ – and make references to the video game Super Mario 
and the television series The Sopranos. In terms of the cultural work they perform, this set 
of memes do several things, including bolstering Mangione’s masculinity and handsome­
ness as an ‘Italian Stallion’, as well as positioning him in relation to discourses of machismo 
CELEBRITY STUDIES
5
Figure 3. Mangione Italian Memes.
6
T. HORECK
that reference a set of stereotypes about Italians as gangsters and criminals. They also 
work to unsettle the assuredness of his whiteness. In an Instagram video post titled 
‘“Light-Skin” Luigi’ from 9 December 2024, the day Mangione was apprehended, news 
personality Reecie Colbert discusses early media references to the shooter suspect as 
‘light-skinned’ and jokes: ‘Sorry Italians, I’ve got some bad news for you . . . that’s why they 
was calling the man light-skinned, this means, Italians, that y’all light-skinned now, y’all 
not white . . . you all have been racially demoted . . . welcome to the BIPOC club!’3 Calling 
out the refusal or inability of some news outlets to use the word ‘White’ when reporting 
on the shooting (which is also discussed in Josh Johnson’s comedy routine), Colbert’s 
video indicates how media constructions of Mangione’s whiteness are shifting and 
unstable.
To understand more fully what happens to the category of whiteness in the sexualisa­
tion of Mangione across multiple internet spaces, it is necessary to turn to a broader 
consideration of the notion of the ‘hot felon’.
Felon fantasies
While the valorisation of murderers as objects of sexual adoration pre-dates digital 
culture, as with the oft-cited example of the fandom around Ted Bundy, the notion of 
the ‘hot felon’ is a decidedly internet phenomenon that emerged in 2014 in relation to 
Jeremy Meeks, a 30-year-old mixed race American man who was arrested on felony 
weapons charges. In the 2010s, there was a period in which it became common for police 
departments in America to post mugshots on their Facebook pages. This practice was 
justified as a digital updating of the ‘bulletin board on the town green’ (Bidgood 2015). 
Although the Stockton Police Department in California had been routinely posting mug 
shots on its Facebook page since 2012, they had not received any special attention until 
18 June 2014, when Meeks’ mugshot went viral because of his ‘beautiful’ looks (Serna and 
Lopez 2014) (Figure 4).
Indicative comments on Meeks’ mug shot include: ‘Hottest criminal ever!’; ‘Let him go – 
he is beautiful’; and ‘America’s Next Top Model’.4 The spread, reach, and ‘stickiness’ of the 
collective refrain – ‘he’s so hot, he should be a model!’ – was such that, upon his release 
from prison, Meeks did indeed become a ‘top model’ when he signed a reported six figure 
deal with the talent agency White Cross Management and became Insta-famous (he now 
Figure 4. Mugshot of Jeremy Meeks, the original ‘hot felon’.
CELEBRITY STUDIES
7
has over a million followers). Meeks eventually married (and then divorced) Chloe Green, 
the billionaire heiress to the Top Shop fortune. In February 2017, Meeks made his runway 
debut during New York Fashion week. Following Meeks’ catwalk appearance, Vogue ran 
an article that rhapsodised about his ‘glinting crystal blue eyes, Greek god-like chiselled 
jaw and lone tear-drop tattoo’ and referred to him as a ‘buff bad boy’ (Satenstein 2017). 
This description, with its references to ‘crystal blue eyes’ and ‘Greek’ looks gives a sense of 
the muddled and veiled ways in which race was discussed in media accounts of Meeks. In 
general, Meeks’ mixed-race identity, with a White mother and a Black father, tended to be 
passed over in the focus on his ‘beauty’; race was only referenced by the media in coded, 
and often racist ways, when his ‘gangland’ past in California as a member of the ‘Crips’ 
gang was brought up (Boshoff 2018).
As noted, Meeks’ ‘hot felony’ was born of the social media age and enabled by the 
platform affordances of Facebook. Significantly, Facebook itself emerged out of a website 
called ‘Facemash’, created by Mark Zuckerberg and his friends in 2003, which invited 
Harvard students to evaluate the relative attractiveness of two different photos of female 
students (Horton 2018). While today Zuckerberg might well try to make light of Facemash 
as a ‘prank’, it is not insignificant that the world’s largest social networking site should 
emerge out of such a ‘hot or not’ ratings system. As Sarah Banet-Weiser argues, ‘the 
prehistory and cultural context of these social network sites are significant because of the 
way in which the sites privilege a specific sort of logic: evaluating and comparing people 
(primarily women)’ (2018, p. 83). ‘Hot or not’ ratings sites function as part of what Banet- 
Weiser describes as a ‘wider culture of commercial hypersexuality’ that normalizes ‘“hot­
ness” as both a category of analysis and an inspirational and aspirational subject position’ 
(2018, pp. 82–83).
Indeed, while it is notably young men who have gone viral as ‘hot felons’, it is young 
women who are the mainstay of websites and accounts devoted to the pornographic 
currency of mugshots. In 2015, the Instagram page and Twitter (now X) account #mug­
shawtys was founded by 24-year-old electrician Josh Jeffery. Branded as ‘hope bae gets 
bail’, #mugshawtys’ Instagram page has 1.9 million followers, and its X account has nearly 
500 thousand followers. #Mugshawtys consists of mugshots of attractive, predominantly 
white women, alongside details of their criminal charges.5 The user comments beneath 
the mugshots rate the sexual attractiveness of the women, with the same joking remarks 
appearing over and over: ‘I can fix her’; ‘She can harass me all she wants’; ‘Only thing she 
drives crazy is me your honor’; 'The only thing she is in Possession of is my HEART your 
honor!'; 'She’s too pretty for jail. Release her'; ‘keep this one, your honor'; ‘Pass’.
Misha Kavka has suggested that, in our ‘highly libidinised media culture’ of 24/7 
consumption via digital screens, ‘The extent to which someone appears in this media 
habitus as “fuckable” – and as a potential fuck-me celebrity – directly aligns with the 
regulatory frameworks of sexual “hotness”, which themselves follow and sustain norms of 
race, age, class, corporeal style, etc’. (2020, p. 17). Online mugshots, I suggest, circulate 
according to the terms of the libidinal celebrity culture described by Kavka. An Instagram 
account such as #Mugshawtys, for example, operates as a kind of loosely satirical site that 
offers up the criminalised face for comedy value and for collective sexual appraisal along 
the lines of the norms referred to by Kavka.
Interestingly, and perhaps not surprisingly, an image of Mangione inside the police 
station in Altoona, Pennsylvania, appears on the #mugshawtys Instagram account. Some 
8
T. HORECK
followers of the account complained that Mangione did not count as a ‘shawty’, implying 
that the intrusion of masculinity into the Instagram grid of attractive women was unwel­
come: ‘This ain’t no shawty’. Others, however, emphatically disagreed: ‘No he is THE 
shawty of the year’ said one user, while another retorted: ‘DID YOU JUST ASSUME 
GENDER?!???’ The power of the category of hotness is such that gender, it would seem, 
is easily subsumed into it. As part of this thread of comments, the administrator of the 
#mugshawtys account noted that he hesitated to post the Mangione pic but ultimately 
did so because he was ‘getting spammed with DMs’ about it. To date, Mangione’s image 
on the account has been liked 106,000 times; it is striking that the 4,362 comments on 
Mangione’s image are not substantively different in tenor from those that are found under 
the female mugshots: ‘#freehim'; 'please take this down, that is my husband'; 'We the jury 
find daddy, I mean the defendant . . . not guilty’; ‘Your honour, free him and give him 
Elon’s home address’.
#Mugshawtys is at the lower, smuttier end of the cultural scale of Mangione’s celeb­
rification, along with the NSFW (not safe for work) images of his purported penis that have 
circulated on the gay internet. Although there has been much disparaging attention 
focused on the heterosexual female fandom surrounding Mangione – one New York Post 
report, for example, describes the women lining up to watch his courtroom hearing as 
‘goo-goo-eyed groupies’ (Schnitzer et al. 2025) and Britain’s The Sun refers to the ‘scream­
ing women’ who idolise him (Crabtree 2025) – Mangione’s rumoured (and totally unver­
ified) queerness has been instrumental to his positioning as a highly sexualised object of 
fascination. Newsweek ran a story on ‘How Luigi Mangione Became a Gay Icon Online’ 
(Lemon 2025) and a range of gay media publications have discussed whether he is 
bisexual, following social media rumours.
The sex talk around Mangione is wild and unfettered, calling to mind Foucault’s 
descriptions in The History of Sexuality of the ‘discursive explosion’ around sex that 
began in the 17th century – ‘the incitement to speak about it, and to do so more and 
more . . . through explicit articulation and endlessly accumulated detail’ (1990, p. 18). 
Reports that Mangione could not have sexual intercourse because of a spine injury are 
found in the same tabloid articles that speculate about the size of his penis and his wild 
sexual exploits with both women and men. An anecdote about how Mangione ‘sent 
women unsolicited nude photos despite a friend claiming he was afraid of porn addiction 
that he believes stops men from meeting real-life women’ (Crabtree 2025) mingles 
alongside remarks from a ‘sex tape broker’ who claims to have seen ‘seedy’ sex videos 
made by Mangione for a gay user on Grindr (Poleo Rodriguez 2025). I suggest that this 
discourse is not about Mangione’s sexuality as an individual but about internet fans 
wanting to have it all ways. The polymorphous nature of the collective desire for 
Mangione is exemplified in the circulation of a Generative AI meme of him as a woman; 
initially, the meme was designed to speak to a straight male’s subject position: ‘If ur 
straight and don’t understand the hype, this is him as a woman’. The assumed hetero­
normativity of this meme was quickly challenged in a queer repurposing by a lesbian 
Instagram site which crossed out the word straight: ‘If ur a lesbian and don’t understand 
the hype, this is him as a woman’ (Figure 5).
In the final section of this essay, I turn my attention to a consideration of the aesthetics 
of the conventional news and police photography of Mangione. This photography pre­
sents Mangione as a prisoner, and frames him as a (guilty) criminal, even as, provocatively, 
CELEBRITY STUDIES
9
to borrow again from the words of Judith Butler, the frame ‘breaks from its context’ (2009, 
p. 10) to thematise something quite different, setting the conditions for his sanctification.
‘The photograph is handsome, as is the boy’
In a powerful and famous passage in Camera Lucida, Roland Barthes shares a photo – the 
‘Portrait of Lewis Payne’, taken by Alexander Gardner, in 1865. Payne tried to assassinate 
Secretary of State W. H. Seward, and the photograph was taken in his cell before he was 
hanged. Barthes writes: ‘The photograph is handsome, as is the boy: that is the studium. 
But the punctum is: he is going to die’ (1993, p. 96). As an embodiment of Barthes’ theories 
on temporality, photography and death, the image captures the ‘horror’ of an ‘anterior 
future of which death is the stake’ (1993, p. 96).
There is a compelling correspondence between the death row photograph of 21-year- 
old Lewis Payne and the extradition photograph of 26-year-old Luigi Mangione, in an 
orange prison jumpsuit, hands similarly cuffed in front of him, flanked by a phalanx of 
police officers (Figure 6). ‘The photograph is handsome, as is the boy’. Certainly, there is as 
much rapture over the beauty and composition of Mangione’s extradition photographs, 
as there is over the handsomeness of his appearance in them. The photograph in Figure 6 
is one of several photographs – and videos – taken during Mangione’s extradition to New 
York City from Pennsylvania on 19 December 2024. They are framed by the media through 
the knowledge that the state will likely pursue public execution, a fact which was 
confirmed on 1 April 2025, when US Attorney General Pam Bondi cited her intention via 
Instagram post6 and Fox TV news interview, to ‘carry out President Trump’s agenda to 
stop violent crime and Make America Safe Again’ by directing federal prosecutors to seek 
the death penalty for Mangione (Halpert and Wendling 2024).
Photographs prefigure death, as in Barthes’ formulation of ‘he is dead and he is 
going to die’, and the media circulation of photographs in the run up to state 
executions also shape and circulate ‘visual knowledge’ about death row prisoners 
(Beckman 2004, p. 12). Beckman draws from the work of John Tagg to make the 
additional, important point that ‘the photograph can never be wholly in the service of 
oppressive regimes and institutions, no matter how strongly aligned with these 
Figure 5. Gender-bending Luigi Mangione memes.
10
T. HORECK
regimes it might seem’ (2004, p. 14). Judith Butler offers a similar argument in her 
discussion of how the ‘frame of the frame’ calls into question the notion of framing, 
and shows that something always exceeds it, troubling the conformity of ‘our estab­
lished understanding of things’ (2009, p. 9). This is to say that the ‘frame does not hold 
anything together in one place, but itself becomes a kind of perpetual breakage’ and 
that ‘what is taken for granted in one instance becomes thematized critically or even 
incredulously in another’ (Butler 2009, p. 10).
The extradition photographs of Mangione are a striking instance of how images can 
‘break out’ and assume different meanings, leading to a ‘new trajectory of affect’ (Butler  
2009, p. 11). This is what happened when the NYPD posted their own photos of 
Mangione’s arrival in New York City via helicopter, and of his walk through the streets 
to the federal courthouse flanked by numerous armed police officers as well as govern­
ment officials including New York Mayor Eric Adams. According to a BBC video report, 
‘officials made an example of him by parading him in front of cameras’.7 ‘Making an 
example of him’ might have been the intention, but the social media response to it was to 
interrogate the ‘theatrics’ of the photographs which were described as looking like the set 
of a Hollywood production or a photoshoot.
In a post for Facebook,8 the American writer and activist Rebecca Solnit compared the 
photograph to Renaissance paintings of Christ, which was echoed elsewhere, as in Ed 
Simon’s Op-Ed post, ‘The Canonization of St. Luigi’: ‘The authorities inadvertently trans­
formed a perp walk photo of the accused killer of a healthcare CEO into a Renaissance 
painting of the arrest of Christ’ (Figure 7). Political columnist and author Nathan J. 
Robinson tweeted the extradition photograph with the following caption: NYPD appar­
ently sat around asking themselves: “how can we arrange to transport Mangione in a way 
that makes him look to his supporters like Christ being taken to the cross’ (Figure 8). The 
point is clear: the state’s attempt to depict Mangione as villainous ‘backfired’ spectacu­
larly, as the photographs taken of him visually reinforce an idea of him as a martyr, a ‘kind 
of prettier Jesus’, as one X user suggested (cited in Simon 2024).
Figure 6. Death Row Photo of Lewis Payne, 1865 and Extradition Photo of Luigi Mangione.
CELEBRITY STUDIES
11
Figure 7. Solnit Facebook post.
12
T. HORECK
Following the circulation of the extradition photographs, memes of Mangione as Christ 
multiplied, along with the sale of merchandise including ‘Saint Luigi’ T-shirts, mugs and 
candles, with some merch sites linking to Mangione’s defence fund page (Figure 9). A 
‘GiveSendGo’ page for Mangione, set up by his lawyers to fund his defence, has raised 
over a million dollars.9 The page includes a photo of the youthful Mangione in court with 
his lawyers, police standing behind them; he is wearing a green sweater with a protective 
vest over it, his head slightly turned to one side as he looks at something out of shot. 
(Figure 10)
The comments of donors in the sidebar of the ‘GiveSendGo’ page are a stream of 
goodwill and positive ‘Free Luigi’ vibes. Some donors indicate that they have contributed 
more than once to the fund. They proclaim Mangione’s innocence and laud his good 
character; many of them also criticise the current political administration in the United 
States and express outrage over the greed and corruption of private healthcare. While 
Figure 8. Posts on ‘X’ mocking the NYPD photos of Mangione’s extradition.
Figure 9. Luigi Mangione as Jesus and Mangione merchandise.
CELEBRITY STUDIES
13
there is not necessarily one coherent political frame through which his case is viewed, 
what is apparent are the goodwill vibes, and affective gathering around Mangione as a 
sympathetic, heroic and saint-like figure.
The question is: where does this affect go – beyond into the coffers of Mangione’s 
defence fund? What cultural work does it perform? The critical issue, for me, is how the 
affective response to Mangione relates to the most compelling aspect of his case, which is 
the public discussion it has raised about what constitutes crime. This discussion is serious 
and important even if it is not always presented in serious fashion and is dressed up 
salaciously as in the outpouring of internet ‘thirst’ over Mangione. The case has initiated 
widespread anger from large swathes of the American public against the for-profit 
healthcare system, which causes death, harm and misery for the non-wealthy American 
populace. In the public discussions that have emerged regarding the murderousness of 
private health care insurance companies in the United States, there is a shift away from a 
focus on criminal individuals and ‘types’ to an interrogation of the violence of corpora­
tions. As filmmaker Michael Moore, and the director of Sicko, a 2007 documentary that 
challenges the greed and injustice of US healthcare, notes:
It is about the mass death and misery – the physical pain, the mental abuse, the medical debt, 
the bankruptcies in the face of denied claims and denied care and bottomless deductibles on 
top of ballooning premiums – that this ‘health care’ industry has levied against the American 
people for decades. With no one standing in their way! Just a government – two broken 
parties – enabling this INDUSTRY’s theft and, yes, murder. (cited in Shoard 2024)
Indeed, while some commentators described the response to the murder of Brian 
Thompson as ‘abhorrent’ and ‘un-American’ (Sonnenfeld and Tian 2024), others noted 
that much of the online vitriol was not about Thompson as an individual but was ‘targeted 
at an industry often seen as a despised fact of life in America’ (Glenza 2024).
In many regards, this recognition of structural violence and murderous systems of 
white corporate power is what critical race theorists and feminists have long called for, 
and it appears to advance understandings of the social contexts of crime and of how a 
Figure 10. Screenshot of Give, Send, Go Page for Mangione defence fund, dated April 24, 2025.
14
T. HORECK
lack of institutionalised care claims lives. However, as this essay has suggested, the 
valorisation of Mangione is not straightforward and in many ways the fervour around 
him as a celebrated criminal also undermines this more collective and structural recogni­
tion of crime, feeding into a cult of the white male individual.
Conclusion: on memes and mugshots
I am mindful of the fact that I write this essay at a time when federal prosecutors have 
formally announced their plans to seek the death penalty for Luigi Mangione. This is the 
first federal death sentence sought by the current Trump administration and it heralds the 
‘shifting politics of capital punishment in the US’ (Wright 2025). As reported by The 
Guardian, ‘Trump’s first term was marked by 13 federal executions in rapid succession – 
more people put to death in the federal system than under the previous 10 presidents 
combined’ and there is now every indication that this second Trump administration will 
pursue and expand capital punishment even further (Levin 2025). The American Civil 
Liberties Union notes that the second Trump administration ‘threatens to accelerate mass 
incarceration, further dehumanize people in our criminal legal system, engage in a death 
penalty “killing spree”, and reverse many reforms gained over the last two decades’ 
(Trump on the Criminal). The fervent celebrification of Mangione as saint and sex object 
is unlikely to thwart the state in its determination to eradicate him as a show of its 
strength and power. But understanding and interrogating the frames through which 
Mangione’s case is interpreted is part of the work that needs to be done to challenge 
the rationales for state violence at a time when it is accelerating.
In conclusion, it is important to note that public understandings of crime and crimin­
ality are being put under serious strain at a historical moment in which the 47th President 
of the United States has a history of reported sexual misconduct and rape allegations and 
is a convicted felon. Indeed, Trump is the first President in US history to have a mugshot 
(Christenson 2023) (Figure 11). Significantly, that mugshot was instantly transformed into 
a badge of honour for Trump and his supporters. Following the publication of his mug­
shot, which was taken at a police station in Atlanta, Georgia on 24 August 2023, Trump’s 
election campaign reported making 7.1 million dollars in profits from its commercialisa­
tion. The BBC reported that much of this money came from ‘merchandise such as mugs, T- 
shirts and drink coolers bearing the former president’s scowling face’ on his mugshot 
(Greenall 2023). A copy of Trump’s mugshot as it appears on the cover of The New York 
Post is framed and hangs just outside the Oval Office (Wise 2025) (Figure 12).
This essay has explored the strange currency of mugshots in a 21st century attention 
economy, arguing that they are central for understanding what is at stake in Luigi 
Mangione’s public adulation, however problematic it may be. Looking at the online 
circulation of Mangione memes, and how they have created a cultural mood of lust and 
sympathy for him, I have argued that his status as a ‘hot felon’ is rooted in the wider 
dynamics of an internet attention economy that commodifies the face as a raced and 
gendered ‘interface-object of celebrity’ (Dinnen and Bean 2022, p. 275).
On 6 May 2025, the date of his 27th birthday, Luigi Mangione wrote a list of ‘27 Things 
he is grateful for’, including his family and friends, and his admirers who send him 
hundreds of letters a day in prison. In the list of 27 things, ‘Memes’ are at number 6. 
Mangione writes: ‘As one of my wisest correspondents put it: “Laughter is louder than 
CELEBRITY STUDIES
15
logic and makes a lot more sense”’ (cited in Thomas 2025). But, as meme scholars such as 
Shifman (2014) have argued, and as this essay has further suggested, there is, in fact, a 
deep cultural logic to internet memes. Their humour belies the role they play in articulat­
ing and creating a cultural lexicon of criminality which determines the kind of bodies and 
crimes that get afforded value and media attention and those which do not, and for that 
reason it is essential that we continue to take them seriously.
